worth_it_explanation: UID2 is The Trade Desk's primary identity solution for addressable targeting as third-party cookies narrow across browsers and environments. An account that has not enabled UID2 targeting, has not mapped its first-party data to UID2, and does not know how much of its targeted supply passes UID2 tokens in the bid stream is bidding with less signal than a properly configured account. These checks are mostly UI inspection: the UID2 settings, the first-party data segment configuration, and the identity coverage breakdowns in reporting. The fixes are configuration changes, not rebuilds.
intro: Unified ID 2.0 (UID2) is an open-source, privacy-conscious identity framework built on hashed and encrypted email addresses and phone numbers. On The Trade Desk, UID2 is the primary way buyers maintain addressable reach on inventory that does not carry third-party cookies, including connected TV (CTV), mobile app, and authenticated publisher environments. European Unified ID (EUID) is the regional variant for European and UK markets, built on the same framework but operating under stricter consent requirements. These checks confirm that the account has UID2 targeting active, that first-party data is mapped to UID2 identifiers so audiences and measurement carry across environments, and that the team understands how much of its targeted supply actually passes UID2 tokens in the bid stream.

audit_checks.title: UID2 targeting is not enabled, leaving the account dependent on cookie-based identifiers
audit_checks.impact_rationale: An account that does not bid on UID2 tokens cannot reach authenticated audiences on CTV, mobile app, or walled-garden-adjacent open web inventory where third-party cookies are absent. As cookie-based reach continues to shrink, the unaddressed share of the target audience grows without any visible signal in standard reach metrics.

educate.base:
When a publisher or supply-side platform passes a bid request through The Trade Desk, it can include a UID2 token alongside or instead of a third-party cookie. UID2 tokens are derived from a user's hashed email address or phone number after the user authenticates on a publisher's site. A buyer that has UID2 targeting enabled can decrypt those tokens, match them against audience segments, and bid on authenticated impressions regardless of whether a third-party cookie is present.

An account that has not enabled UID2 targeting treats those bid requests as anonymous. The targeting logic falls back to contextual signals or probabilistic identifiers, and the account cannot match first-party audiences against authenticated users on UID2-enabled inventory. On CTV and mobile app environments, where third-party cookies have never existed, UID2 is the primary addressability path.

The UID2 overview describes the framework. The UID2 overview for DSPs explains how The Trade Desk and other demand-side platforms decrypt tokens and apply them in the bid stream. For European campaigns, EUID applies the same framework under EU and UK consent requirements; if the account runs campaigns targeting European audiences, EUID adoption is a separate configuration point to confirm.

A few signs point to UID2 targeting being off or incomplete:
  • The account's identity settings do not show UID2 as an active identifier type
  • CTV and mobile app campaigns report very low match rates against first-party audience segments
  • The account has no record of accepting the UID2 participation terms
You may want to confirm UID2 is enabled at the account level before reviewing audience match rates. A match rate problem and an enablement problem look similar in reporting but have different fixes.

site_type_overlays.ecommerce: Retail advertisers with authenticated checkout flows are among the strongest use cases for UID2. The email captured at purchase is already the data type UID2 is built on. If the account runs prospecting or retargeting against known purchasers on open web or CTV, UID2 enablement determines whether those audiences reach authenticated impressions.
site_type_overlays.lead_gen: Lead-generation advertisers that capture email at form submission can map that data to UID2 for retargeting and suppression. Without UID2 enabled, a suppression list built from known leads cannot match against authenticated impressions, and the account may bid on users it has already converted.

investigate.intro: Confirm that UID2 is enabled as an active identifier type in the account settings, and note whether EUID is separately enabled for European campaigns.

the_trade_desk_ui.label: The Trade Desk UI
steps: Navigate to account-level settings and locate the identity or data management section. Confirm whether UID2 is listed as an active identifier type.

If the account runs campaigns targeting European audiences, confirm whether EUID is separately enabled. EUID uses the same technical framework as UID2 but requires its own participation agreement and consent-compliant data handling.

Open a CTV or mobile app ad group and inspect the audience targeting configuration. If first-party segments show zero or near-zero estimated reach, UID2 may be inactive even if the setting appears on.

Review any available identity or reach diagnostics in the platform. The Trade Desk surfaces identity match statistics in some reporting views; a match rate of zero on authenticated inventory confirms the identifier is not in use.

evaluate.logic: The practitioner records whether UID2 is active in the account's identity settings, whether EUID is active for European campaigns (if applicable), and whether CTV or mobile app ad groups show non-zero match rates against first-party audiences. An account with UID2 active, terms accepted, and a measurable match rate on authenticated inventory passes. An account with UID2 off, or on but with zero match rates on authenticated inventory, is a fail recorded with the affected campaign types.
evaluate.fallback_message: Record the UID2 enablement status, the EUID status for European campaigns if applicable, and the identity match rate observation for authenticated inventory types above to see the evaluation.

generate.narrative_template: {{voice_found}} that UID2 targeting is not enabled on {{site_name}}'s The Trade Desk account, leaving the account dependent on third-party cookies for audience addressability. Inventory that does not carry third-party cookies, including CTV and mobile app, is unreachable with first-party audience segments under this configuration. {{voice_recommend}} enabling UID2 at the account level, accepting the participation terms, and confirming that European campaigns also have EUID active if they target EU or UK users. After enabling, {{voice_recommend}} verifying the identity match rate on authenticated inventory types to confirm the identifier is working in the bid stream.

audit_checks.title: First-party data is not mapped to UID2, so audiences and measurement do not carry across cookie-free environments
audit_checks.impact_rationale: First-party data that exists only as a cookie-based segment cannot reach authenticated users on CTV, mobile app, or browsers where third-party cookies are blocked. Retargeting and suppression lists built on that data underdeliver on a growing share of inventory, and the conversion measurement that relies on matching known users suffers the same gap.

educate.base:
Enabling UID2 at the account level is a precondition for UID2 targeting, but it does not automatically make first-party audience segments UID2-addressable. The advertiser's customer data, whether CRM lists, site visitors captured by the Universal Pixel, or purchase data, has to be explicitly mapped to UID2 identifiers before those segments can match against authenticated bid requests.

The mapping process involves hashing the advertiser's email addresses or phone numbers and sending them to The Trade Desk's UID2 operator, which returns the corresponding UID2 identifiers. The UID2 advertiser and data provider integration guide describes this workflow. The Trade Desk also supports direct segment uploads that go through the mapping process automatically when the data is formatted correctly.

A segment that was built before UID2 mapping was configured, or that is continuously populated from a source that has not been connected to the UID2 workflow, contains only cookie-based identifiers. On inventory where third-party cookies are absent, the segment matches zero users, and the targeting or suppression logic has no effect.

A few signs point to the mapping gap:
  • First-party segments show strong estimated reach on desktop web but near-zero reach on CTV or mobile app
  • Suppression lists built from known converters do not reduce delivery to those users on CTV
  • The data management configuration does not show a UID2 mapping step or a connected UID2 data provider
You may want to compare segment reach estimates across environment types. A segment with healthy desktop reach and negligible CTV or app reach is a reliable signal that the underlying identifiers are cookie-based only.

site_type_overlays.ecommerce: Purchase data and cart-abandonment segments are the highest-value first-party lists for an e-commerce advertiser. If those lists have not been mapped to UID2, retargeting and suppression against known purchasers works only on the share of desktop web inventory where a third-party cookie is present. CTV retargeting of known customers requires UID2-mapped data.
site_type_overlays.lead_gen: A lead suppression list that prevents known leads from seeing top-of-funnel ads depends on the suppression data matching the same identifier that appears in the bid request. Without UID2 mapping, suppression works on cookie-based inventory but not on CTV or mobile app, where leads who converted on desktop may continue to see acquisition ads.

learn_more.source_title: Advertiser and data provider integration guide

investigate.intro: Confirm whether the account's first-party segments have been mapped to UID2 identifiers, and compare segment reach estimates across environment types to surface any cookie-only segments.

the_trade_desk_ui.label: The Trade Desk UI
steps: Open the data management or audience library section of the account. For each first-party segment used in active campaigns, note whether the segment source indicates UID2 mapping has been applied.

Select a high-value retargeting or suppression segment and review its reach breakdown by environment type, for example desktop, CTV, and mobile app, if that breakdown is available. A segment with substantial desktop reach but near-zero CTV or mobile app reach is a signal that the underlying identifiers are cookie-based.

Review the data onboarding or segment upload configuration. Confirm whether the workflow includes a UID2 mapping step, either through a direct integration with The Trade Desk's UID2 operator or through a data partner that handles the mapping.

Check whether any segments are populated by the Universal Pixel without a UID2 mapping step. Universal Pixel audiences built on page-visit events carry the cookie-based identifier by default unless UID2 mapping is explicitly configured.

evaluate.logic: The practitioner records, per high-value first-party segment, whether UID2 mapping is confirmed in the data configuration and whether the segment shows non-trivial reach on CTV or mobile app. A segment with confirmed UID2 mapping and measurable cross-environment reach passes. A segment with cookie-only identifiers, evidenced by near-zero reach on authenticated inventory types, is a fail recorded with the segment name and the environments where reach drops to zero.
evaluate.fallback_message: Record the UID2 mapping status and the cross-environment reach observation for each first-party segment reviewed above to see the evaluation.

generate.narrative_template: {{voice_found}} that first-party audience segments on {{site_name}}'s The Trade Desk account have not been mapped to UID2 identifiers. The segments contain cookie-based identifiers that do not match authenticated users on CTV, mobile app, or browsers where third-party cookies are absent, so retargeting and suppression logic underdelivers on a meaningful share of the targeted supply. {{voice_recommend}} mapping the account's first-party data to UID2 through The Trade Desk's data onboarding workflow or a connected data partner, then confirming that high-value segments show non-trivial reach on CTV and mobile app before relaunching the affected campaigns.

audit_checks.title: Identity coverage across targeted supply is not understood, so the account does not know how much inventory is UID2-addressable
audit_checks.impact_rationale: Without visibility into what share of targeted impressions carry UID2 tokens, the account cannot tell whether its identity investment is working or whether it is effectively bidding blind on a large slice of its own spend. A campaign that looks fully configured for UID2 may still serve a majority of its impressions on cookie-only supply where UID2 has no effect.

educate.base:
Not all supply that runs through The Trade Desk passes UID2 tokens in bid requests. UID2 adoption varies by publisher, supply-side platform, and environment type. CTV and authenticated news publishers tend to have higher UID2 coverage; open web display inventory from publishers without authentication flows has lower coverage. An account running campaigns across a mix of these environments may find that a meaningful share of its impressions receive no UID2 token at all, even when the account is fully configured.

The Trade Desk's identity reporting and the OpenPath direct-publisher integrations can surface how much of the targeted supply is passing UID2 tokens. Reviewing this breakdown helps the team understand whether the identity investment is reaching the inventory where it matters and whether budget allocation toward UID2-heavy environments, such as CTV or premium authenticated publishers via OpenPath, is worth considering.

The UID2 DSP integration guide describes how DSPs receive and process UID2 tokens in the bid stream, which provides context for interpreting coverage data. A few signs point to the coverage picture being unknown:
  • The account has never reviewed identity match or UID2 token coverage broken down by supply source or environment
  • Campaign reporting does not include an identity or addressability dimension
  • The team assumes UID2 is working because it is enabled, without verifying coverage on the actual supply the campaigns are buying
You may want to pull the identity coverage breakdown at least once per major campaign flight to confirm the supply mix is consistent with the account's addressability expectations.

site_type_overlays.ecommerce: Retail campaigns that target high-intent audiences across CTV and open web benefit most from understanding the UID2 coverage split. If the retargeting segment is UID2-mapped but the targeted supply is predominantly cookie-only display, the UID2 work has limited impact. Shifting budget toward higher-coverage environments may improve addressable reach more than further refinement of the segment itself.
site_type_overlays.lead_gen: Lead-generation campaigns that rely on suppression lists to control frequency against known leads need UID2-passing supply for that suppression to work. Knowing what share of the campaign's supply passes UID2 tokens tells the team how much of the frequency control is actually in effect.

investigate.intro: Review the identity or addressability reporting available in the account to understand what share of targeted supply passes UID2 tokens, broken down by environment and supply source where possible.

the_trade_desk_ui.label: The Trade Desk UI
steps: Open the reporting section and look for an identity or addressability report type. The Trade Desk surfaces UID2 token coverage in some reporting views; the availability of this breakdown depends on the account's reporting configuration.
If an identity breakdown is available, note the share of impressions that carried a UID2 token versus those that did not, broken down by environment type: CTV, mobile app, and desktop web.

Review which supply sources contribute the most cookie-only impressions. OpenPath-direct publishers generally have higher UID2 coverage than open exchange supply; comparing the two can help identify whether shifting budget toward direct supply would improve addressable reach.
If the account uses a third-party measurement or identity partner, check whether their reporting surfaces UID2 match rates alongside cookie-based reach to give a more complete coverage picture.

evaluate.logic: The practitioner records whether identity coverage data is available in reporting, and if so, what share of targeted impressions carried a UID2 token across CTV, mobile app, and desktop web. An account where coverage data is available and shows non-trivial UID2 reach on the supply types the campaigns target passes. An account where coverage data is unavailable, or where UID2 token coverage is near zero across all environment types despite UID2 being enabled, is a fail recorded with the affected campaigns and the environments where coverage is absent.
evaluate.fallback_message: Record the UID2 token coverage breakdown by environment type, or note that the breakdown is unavailable in current reporting, above to see the evaluation.

generate.narrative_template: {{voice_found}} that {{site_name}}'s The Trade Desk account does not have a clear picture of UID2 identity coverage across its targeted supply. The account may be configured for UID2 but still serving a large share of impressions on inventory that passes no UID2 token, which means the identity investment has limited effect on those impressions. {{voice_recommend}} pulling an identity or addressability coverage report broken down by environment type, reviewing which supply sources contribute the most cookie-only impressions, and considering whether rebalancing budget toward higher-coverage environments such as CTV or OpenPath-direct publishers would improve addressable reach across the campaign mix.
